perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees that it shall not, and shall cause it Representative not to, remove, overprint or deface any notice of confidentiality, copyright, t rademark, logo, legend or other notices of ownership or confidentiality from any original or copies of the Confidential Information it obtains from Disclosing Party, or authorize any third party to do any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees that it shall endeavor not to, and shall encourage its Representatives not to, remove, overprint, or deface any notice of confidentiality, copyright, trademark, logo, legend, or other notices of ownership or confidentiality from any original or copies of the Confidential Information it obtains from Disclosing Party, or authorize any third party to do any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' and 'shall cause' to 'shall encourage' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a guarantee, and 'encourage' is less forceful than 'cause.' This creates ambiguity about the Receiving Party's responsibility to prevent such actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Copyright Act, 17 U.S. Code § 506 - Criminal offenses
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 506 protects copyright notices. The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to prevent defacement of these notices, potentially making it more difficult to pursue legal action if a copyright notice is removed, since the contract no longer creates a clear obligation.

Criminal offenses (a) Criminal Infringement.- (1) In general .-Any person who willfully infringes a copyright shall be punished as provided under section 2319 of title 18 , if the infringement was committed- (A) for purposes of commercial advantage or private financial gain; (B) by the reproduction or distribution, including by electronic means, during any 180–day period, of 1 or more copies or phonorecords of 1 or more copyrighted works, which have a total retail value of more than $1
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to prevent the defacement of copyright notices. This contradicts the implied protection of copyright notices as suggested by 17 U.S.C. § 506, which addresses criminal offenses related to copyright infringement. While the snippet provided does not explicitly state the requirement to prevent defacement, it implies that willful infringement is a violation. By weakening the obligation to prevent defacement, the modified text increases the risk of infringement and makes it more difficult to enforce the protection of copyright notices, thus creating a legal contradiction as an ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Conf idential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall s urvive and continue for five (5) years after the date of the last discl osure of Confidential Informati on by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall generally survive for approximately five (5) years after the date of the last disclosure of Confidential Information by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall survive and continue for five (5) years' to 'shall generally survive for approximately five (5) years' introduces ambiguity. 'Generally' and 'approximately' make the duration of confidentiality vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-701 - Agreements required to be in writing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York GOL § 5-701 requires certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and clearly defined. The ambiguity introduced regarding the survival period of the confidentiality obligations weakens the enforceability of the agreement under New York law, as the terms are no longer definite.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the survival period of confidentiality obligations by using the terms 'generally' and 'approximately'. This directly weakens the enforceability of the agreement. New York GOL § 5-701, as shown in both scraped law snippets, requires agreements that cannot be performed within one year to be in writing. The original text provided a definite five-year survival period, while the modified text weakens this, potentially making the agreement unenforceable because the duration is no longer clearly defined, thus contradicting the requirement for agreements to be clearly defined when they extend beyond one year.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of any Confidential Information, Receiving Part y shall: (a) only disclose such Confidential Information to those of its Representatives with a need to have access to and knowledge of the Confidential Inform ation for the limited purpose expressly stated herein and not disclose to any other person ex cept with the prior written approval of Disclosing Party
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of any Confidential Information, Receiving Party should aim to: (a) disclose such Confidential Information only to those of its Representatives who might require access to the Confidential Information for the purpose stated herein and try not to disclose to any other person except with the approval of Disclosing Party
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should aim to' and 'need to have access to' to 'might require access' weakens the mandatory nature of the clause. Adding 'try not to' before 'disclose' further reduces the binding nature of the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996, 18 U.S.C. § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1832 addresses the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to restrict access to confidential information, the modification increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prove that the Receiving Party took adequate steps to prevent theft of trade secrets, which is a relevant factor in pursuing legal action under the Economic Espionage Act.
perturbation.justification: The original contract uses mandatory language ('shall'), while the changed version uses suggestive language ('should aim to' and 'try not to'), weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. The Law Explanation indicates that this weakening could hinder the Disclosing Party's ability to prove adequate steps were taken to prevent theft of trade secrets under 18 U.S.C. § 1832, which is relevant to the Economic Espionage Act. Snippet 2 references the Economic Espionage Act, though it provides no direct language. Although the scraped snippets do not directly define the mandatory measures required, the Law Explanation points out how diminishing the protective measures in the contract negatively affects compliance with regulations against trade secret theft.

perturbation.original_text: At any time upon written demand by Disclosing Party, Receivi ng Party shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, immedi ately return (or, at the op tion of Disclosing Party, destroy) all tangible or electronic Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving P arty or any of its Representatives under this Agreement, along with all copies, extracts and reproductio ns of such information and all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, specifications, designs, plans , enhancements, data, prototypes, derivative works and all other works prepared by Receiving Party or any of its Representatives that are derived from or based on any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon reasonable written demand by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall endeavor, and shall encourage its Representatives, to return (or, at the option of Disclosing Party, reasonably destroy) most tangible or electronic Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party or any of its Representatives under this Agreement, along with copies and extracts of such information and notes derived from or based on any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. Replacing 'shall' with 'shall endeavor,' 'cause' with 'encourage,' and 'immediately' with an implied 'reasonable' timeframe, along with specifying 'most' and limiting the scope of what must be returned to 'copies and extracts of such information and notes,' creates significant ambiguity. 'Reasonably destroy' creates uncertainty, and excluding other listed items reduces the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by New York), N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 350 protects against the misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon demand, making it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to prevent the ongoing use or disclosure of trade secrets and, therefore, hindering their ability to enforce their rights under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the 'Receiving Party shall immediately return...all tangible or electronic Confidential Information'. The modified text weakens this obligation by changing 'shall' to 'shall endeavor', 'cause' to 'encourage', and limiting the scope of returnable information to 'most tangible or electronic Confidential Information' and 'copies and extracts'. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the legal force of the obligation to protect trade secrets. Although the provided snippet is not directly related to misappropriation of trade secrets, the law explanation states the weakening of the Receiving Party's obligation hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce their rights under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The original text is very direct, making compliance relatively straightforward. The changes introduce subjectivity and optionality, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, th e prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses incurred in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, the prevailing party may be entitled to attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses, as deemed appropriate by the court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees' to 'may be entitled to attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses, as deemed appropriate by the court' introduces significant ambiguity. The word 'may' makes the awarding of fees discretionary rather than mandatory, and adding 'as deemed appropriate by the court' further weakens the entitlement, placing it entirely at the court's discretion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 5001 - Interest to verdict, report or decision
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY CPLR § 5001 primarily concerns interest, it highlights the importance of clear and predictable remedies in legal actions. By making attorneys' fees discretionary, the modified text undermines the principle that a prevailing party should be fully compensated for their losses. This introduces uncertainty and reduces the effectiveness of the contract as a deterrent to breach.

Interest to verdict, report or decision. (a) Actions in which recoverable. Interest shall be recovered upon a sum awarded because of a breach of performance of a contract, or because of an act or omission depriving or otherwise interfering with title to, or possession or enjoyment of, property, except that in an action of an equitable nature, interest and the rate and date from which it shall be computed shall be in the court's discretion. (b) Date from which computed. Interest shall be computed from the earliest ascertainable date the cause of action existed, except that interest upon damages incurred thereafter shall be computed from the date incurred. Where such damages were incurred at various times, interest shall be computed upon each item from the date it was incurred or upon all of the damages from a single reasonable intermediate date. (c) Specifying date; computing interest. The date from which interest is to be computed shall be specified in the verdict, report or decision. If a jury is discharged without specifying the date, the court upon motion shall fix the date, except that where the date is certain and not in dispute, the date may be fixed by the clerk of the court upon affidavit. The amount of interest shall be computed by the clerk of the court, to the date the verdict was rendered or the report or decision was made, and included in the total sum awarded.

perturbation.justification: The original text states the prevailing party 'shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees,' creating a clear legal entitlement. The changed text modifies this to 'may be entitled to attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses, as deemed appropriate by the court,' introducing ambiguity and discretion where there was none. This undermines the certainty of remedy for breach of contract. While the law snippets provided (NY CPLR § 5001) primarily concern interest, they emphasize the principle of predictable remedies in breach of contract cases. The modification directly contradicts this principle by making the award of attorneys' fees discretionary, effectively weakening the contract's ability to provide full compensation to the prevailing party as is expected in legal proceedings.
